---
## ---- JSS submission metadata --------------------------------------
## Structured in JSS conventions, following ../../fbrglm/paper/. The
## active build target is `html_document` (development build); the JSS
## submission build (rticles::jss_article) operates on a temporary copy
## via paper/render.sh and reuses this metadata.
## --------------------------------------------------------------------

title: "FbSQL: A Closure-Preserving Formula-based Extension for Statistical Modeling in SQL"
## For rticles::jss_article, replace the line above with the structured
## form below:
## title:
##   formatted: "FbSQL: A Closure-Preserving Formula-based Extension for Statistical Modeling in SQL"
##   plain:     "FbSQL: A Closure-Preserving Formula-based Extension for Statistical Modeling in SQL"
##   short:     "FbSQL: Formula-based Statistical Modeling in SQL"

author:
  - name: "Koki Tsuyuzaki"
    affiliation: "Chiba University`\\\\`{=latex} and RIKEN"
    affiliation2: "Data Science Core, Chiba University`\\\\`{=latex} Chiba, Japan`\\\\[6pt]`{=latex} Omics AI Research Team, Advanced General Intelligence for Science Program (AGIS), RIKEN TRIP Headquarters`\\\\`{=latex} Wako, Japan"
    address: "`%`{=latex}"
    email: "koki.tsuyuzaki@gmail.com"
  - name: "Kentaro Sakamaki"
    affiliation: "Juntendo University`\\\\`{=latex} and Chiba University"
    affiliation2: "Faculty of Health Data Science, Juntendo University`\\\\`{=latex} Tokyo, Japan`\\\\[6pt]`{=latex} Center for Next Generation of Community Health, Chiba University`\\\\`{=latex} Chiba, Japan"
    address: "`%`{=latex}"
    email: "kentaro.sakamaki@gmail.com"
  - name: "Hiromu Nishiuchi"
    affiliation: "Chiba University`\\\\`{=latex} and Chuo University"
    affiliation2: "Data Science Core, Chiba University`\\\\`{=latex} Chiba, Japan`\\\\[6pt]`{=latex} Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Chuo University`\\\\`{=latex} Tokyo, Japan"
    address: "`%`{=latex}"
    email: "hiromunishiuchi@gmail.com"
## Author list, order, affiliations, and corresponding author (first
## author, as in fbrglm) follow the fbrglm JSS draft
## (../../fbrglm/paper/fbrglm-jss-draft.Rmd).

abstract: >
  TODO: abstract. One paragraph: the problem (SQL-ML systems trade away
  SQL design principles), the proposal (FbSQL: a closure-preserving,
  formula-based DSL; glm as proof of concept), the evidence (R-parity
  PoC on PostgreSQL; design comparison with MADlib / PostgresML / Spark
  MLlib / Hivemall / H2O), and the claim (language design, not a faster
  glm).

## keywords TODO: e.g. SQL, PostgreSQL, generalized linear models,
## formula interface, closure, relational model, PL/R

output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

<!-- TODO (1-3 lines when writing): motivate "statistical modeling written
     SQL-like"; existing SQL-ML trades away SQL design principles via model
     objects / procedural interfaces; contributions list (language design,
     relation design, PoC extension). -->

# Related Work

Systems that bring statistical or machine learning computation to SQL users
differ less in raw capability than in which properties of SQL they choose
to preserve. This section reviews five open-source systems through the lens
of the five design principles of the previous section, asking of each
system what it emphasizes and what it trades away — not which system is
better. The review rests on two kinds of evidence, and we keep them
distinct throughout. For Apache MADlib (1.21.0 on PostgreSQL 11),
PostgresML (2.7.12 on its bundled PostgreSQL 15), and Spark MLlib (3.5.1),
the observations are *measured*: we reproduced this paper's running example
— a customer-churn logistic regression — on each system inside pinned
Docker environments in the companion repository `FbSQL-experiments`. For
Apache Hivemall and H2O-3 with Sparkling Water, the review is *literature
based*, drawn from the official documentation and recorded claim by claim
with sources in the companion repository; these systems were not executed,
and we do not report anything their documentation does not state. A full
comparison across nineteen design dimensions is maintained as
version-controlled data (`data/related_work.csv`) in `FbSQL-experiments`
and rendered from it; the present section confines itself to the design
trade-offs and delegates the dimension-by-dimension detail to that table.

<!-- TODO(table wiring): import the condensed comparison table as
     tables/related_work.tex (generated in FbSQL-experiments) and replace
     the prose pointer above with a numbered Table cross-reference. -->

## A taxonomy of SQL-oriented machine learning systems

The systems fall into three groups according to where the modeling
computation lives relative to the SQL language.

**In-database SQL-ML.** Apache MADlib [@hellerstein2012madlib] and
PostgresML [@postgresml] are PostgreSQL extensions: training and prediction
are invoked as SQL function calls, and the computation runs inside the
database server. These systems share FbSQL's host environment and
invocation surface, which makes the differences in language design the most
directly comparable.

**SQL-on-engine ML.** Apache Spark MLlib [@meng2016mllib] and Apache
Hivemall [@hivemall] attach machine learning to distributed query engines
whose query language is, or includes, SQL. In Spark, SQL defines the input
relations (`spark.sql`) while the modeling itself lives in the
DataFrame/Pipeline API beside the SQL dialect; in Hivemall, training and
prediction are user-defined functions written into HiveQL queries, so the
user never leaves SQL, although — as discussed below — much of the
statistical semantics is left to the query author. Hivemall was retired
from the Apache Incubator in September 2022; we review it nonetheless
because its design occupies a distinctive point that no active system
covers.

**SQL-adjacent ML.** H2O-3 with Sparkling Water [@h2o] is a separate
machine learning cluster with its own frame data structure. Sparkling Water
bridges Spark to it, so data defined by Spark SQL can reach H2O — but only
by conversion from a DataFrame into an H2OFrame, after which the modeling
API is R, Python, or Flow rather than SQL. We include it as the boundary
case: a system that SQL users can reach, built without SQL as a design
target.

BigQuery ML [@bigqueryml] also offers model training through SQL
statements, but it is a proprietary managed service rather than open-source
software: its environment cannot be pinned, versioned, and re-executed the
way the systems above can, so we exclude it from the comparison and return
to it only briefly in the *Discussion* section.

## Design trade-offs

**Apache MADlib** emphasizes in-database scale and statistical output: its
`glm()` covers five families with link options, reports standard errors,
*z* statistics, and *p* values, and runs inside PostgreSQL and Greenplum.
The trade-offs concentrate in specification and model representation.
There is no formula: independent variables are supplied as an SQL array
expression string, and categorical predictors must be one-hot encoded by
the user before training, so the declarative-specification work is
delegated to the query author. Training is procedure-style — a
void-returning call that writes two output tables by side effect — and the
model table stores coefficients, standard errors, and *p* values as
parallel arrays without term names, so position is the only link between a
coefficient and its meaning. In our measured runs, the coefficients matched
R's `glm()` to four decimals, but scoring a factor level unseen at training
time produced a silent prediction as the reference level: with user-side
encoding, all the hand-built dummies of an unknown level are zero, and the
system has no fit-time level information against which to detect the
condition.

**PostgresML** shares FbSQL's deployment surface — it is likewise a
PostgreSQL extension called through SQL functions — but pursues a different
objective: a modern machine learning platform with model lifecycle
management. Specification reduces to a task and an algorithm name
(`'classification'`, `'linear'`); there is no family/link concept, and the
reporting consists of machine learning metrics rather than standard errors
or confidence intervals. The fitted model is a serialized binary in
PostgresML's catalog tables, and prediction is a scalar function that
refers to a *project name*, resolving through the currently deployed model
— an indirection through mutable state rather than a value the caller
passes. In our measured runs, text columns were label-encoded to ordinal
reals by default (`F` = 1, `M` = 2, `Other` = 3), which imposes an
artificial ordering inside linear models, and a NULL feature at prediction
time raised a hard error. The emphasis is operational — automatic
train/test splits, metric tracking, deployment — and the trade-off is that
neither the statistical model nor its representation is expressed
relationally.

**Spark MLlib** is the system that demonstrates formula-shaped
specification at scale: its `RFormula` transformer accepts
`churn_flag ~ age + gender` and derives the encoding automatically.
However, the semantics differ from R's — in our measured runs, factor
levels were ordered by frequency with the least frequent level as the
reference, so the fitted model was the same (predictions matched R and
FbSQL to four decimals) while the coefficient table was comparable only
after reparameterization, a hazard when porting R workflows. Closure is
preserved at the DataFrame level rather than the SQL level:
`transform()` maps the input DataFrame to the same DataFrame with appended
prediction columns, which is exactly the relation-in/relation-out shape,
but expressed in the host-language Pipeline API while the fitted model
itself remains a JVM object persisted as a directory. NULL handling and
unseen-level handling are coupled into a single `handleInvalid` switch
whose default raises an error and whose `skip` option silently drops rows,
changing the granularity of the output — there is no keep-the-row-with-NULL
option.

**Apache Hivemall** (literature based) makes the opposite trade-off from
Spark: everything stays inside HiveQL, and the trained model is a genuine
`(feature, weight)` table — the closest any reviewed system comes to
relational closure. What it delegates is the statistical semantics. There
is no family abstraction (training functions are per-algorithm, such as
`logress` for logistic regression); features are arrays of `index:value`
strings assembled by the user, so encoding is manual; the feature column of
the model table is an index without term semantics; and prediction is
hand-written SQL — exploding the feature array, joining the model table,
and applying the link function explicitly. Hyperparameters are packed into
a single option string rather than named arguments. Hivemall thus preserves
the relational mechanics while relying on the query author for everything
the formula machinery would otherwise derive.

**H2O-3 with Sparkling Water** (literature based) is statistically the
richest GLM implementation among the systems reviewed: ten families,
multiple links, offsets, observation weights, and interaction terms are all
documented parameters, with standard errors and *p* values available on
request. It is not a SQL DSL and does not aim to be one: models are
specified as x/y column lists rather than formulas, the fitted model is a
binary object in the H2O cluster (exportable as MOJO/POJO), and Spark SQL
reaches the system only through DataFrame-to-H2OFrame conversion, at which
point the data has left the relational world. Its documented defaults are
tuned to predictive machine learning rather than to SQL or R conventions:
missing values are mean-imputed by default rather than dropped, default
fits are elastic-net regularized (so unpenalized `glm()` parity requires
setting `lambda = 0`), and factor levels unseen at scoring time are
replaced by the most frequent training level. Within its own design
objective these are coherent choices; they simply answer to a different set
of principles than the ones considered here.

Across the five systems a pattern emerges: each preserves some of the five
principles and relaxes others, and in every case the relaxation follows
from the system's objective — distributed scale for MADlib, lifecycle
operations for PostgresML, pipeline composition for Spark, staying inside
HiveQL for Hivemall, and statistical breadth for H2O. Notably, closure
appears twice in partial form — at the DataFrame level in Spark and in the
model-as-table of Hivemall — but in no reviewed system do set-oriented
invocation, declarative formula semantics, and relation-in/relation-out
closure hold simultaneously at the SQL level.

## Positioning of FbSQL

FbSQL differs from these systems in its design objective rather than in
capability. It asks what the conjunction looks like: SQL functions inside a
DBMS (as in MADlib and PostgresML), model specification with R's formula
semantics (which Spark approximates in shape but not in semantics), a
fitted model that *is* a relation consumed and produced by ordinary queries
(which Hivemall approaches mechanically but without term semantics), and
model metadata that travels inside that relation rather than residing in
system catalogs or host-language objects. Each element exists somewhere in
the reviewed systems; to our knowledge, no system offers their combination
under the five principles, and the running example of this paper is
designed to make the resulting differences observable rather than
rhetorical. The price of this objective is scope: FbSQL's proof of concept
covers two families and deliberately omits offsets, weights, and
interactions — exclusions we return to in the *Discussion* section. The
systems reviewed here are not deficient versions of FbSQL, and FbSQL is not
a replacement for any of them; they optimize different points in a design
space that this paper tries to make explicit.

# Design Principles

FbSQL starts from a deliberately narrow question: if statistical modeling
is to be written *in* SQL, rather than merely invoked *from* SQL, which
properties of the language must be preserved? The contribution is therefore
not the ability to call R's `glm()` from a database — PL/R [@plr] has
provided embedded R in PostgreSQL for many years, and FbSQL indeed uses it
internally — but a language design in which fitting and prediction obey the
same principles as every other SQL operation. This section states the five
principles that FbSQL adopts as design constraints. For each one we describe
its meaning in SQL, the tension it creates with conventional statistical
modeling interfaces, the FbSQL specification decision that resolves that
tension, and the different trade-offs made by existing SQL-oriented machine
learning systems. Throughout, `fit_glm()` and `predict_glm()` serve as the
proof of concept: none of the principles is specific to generalized linear
models, and the *Discussion* section sketches how the same constraints
would shape future functions such as `fit_rf()`, `fit_lgbm()`, and
`fit_xgb()`.

## Set orientation

SQL operates on relations as wholes: a query is a mapping between sets of
rows, and the language provides no construct for visiting rows one at a
time [@codd1970relational]. Statistical modeling interfaces are typically
object-centric instead — fitting produces a model value, and prediction is
a method invoked on that value, with the iteration over observations hidden
in the host language. When such interfaces are transplanted into SQL, the
row-at-a-time perspective tends to resurface on the prediction side: Apache
MADlib [@hellerstein2012madlib] and PostgresML [@postgresml] both expose
prediction as scalar functions evaluated once per row over a hand-supplied
feature array, so the user, not the system, is responsible for assembling
each observation into the form the model expects.

FbSQL keeps both halves of the modeling workflow set-oriented. `fit_glm()`
consumes a relation — any SQL query — and returns its result as a relation
of coefficient rows. `predict_glm()` consumes two relations, the fitted
model and the data to be scored, and returns a relation at the granularity
of the scored data. There is no per-row prediction call and no user-managed
feature vector; the unit of every operation is a relation.

## Declarative specification

SQL describes the result to be computed, not the procedure that computes
it. Model specification in existing SQL-oriented systems is frequently
operational: MADlib takes the independent variables as an SQL array
expression string, and Apache Hivemall [@hivemall] trains on feature arrays
of `index:value` strings that the user assembles by hand, so encoding
categorical variables, constructing interaction columns, and keeping column
order consistent with the coefficients are all user responsibilities.

FbSQL adopts the formula notation of @chambers1992statistical as its model
specification language: `'churn_flag ~ age + gender'` names a statistical
relationship, and the system derives everything operational from it —
factor levels, treatment contrasts, and the construction of the design
matrix — following the same conventions as R's `glm()`. Spark MLlib
[@meng2016mllib] demonstrates that formula-shaped specification is
compatible with a distributed DataFrame system through its `RFormula`
transformer, although with semantics that differ from R's (for example,
frequency-based rather than alphabetical reference levels). PostgresML
makes a different trade-off again: specification reduces to a task and an
algorithm name, with every non-label column of the named relation treated
as a feature — concise, but the statistical model itself is no longer
something the user states.

## Closure: relation in, relation out

Relational algebra is closed: every operator consumes relations and
produces a relation, which is what makes queries composable through
subqueries, views, and common table expressions [@codd1970relational].
This is where statistical modeling diverges most sharply from SQL
practice. In nearly every system the fitted model is an opaque value that
lives outside the data model: an S object in R, a `PipelineModel` in Spark
(a JVM object graph persisted as a directory), a serialized binary model in
PostgresML's catalog tables, or, in MADlib, a pair of side-effect output
tables written by a void-returning training procedure. Once the model is
not a relation, composition stops at the model boundary: the fitted object
can only be handled by the system's own dedicated functions.

The central design decision of FbSQL is that **the fitted model is a
relation**. `fit_glm()` returns a single relation with one row per design
matrix column (`term`, `estimate`, `std_error`, `statistic`, `p_value`,
and Wald confidence bounds) together with model-level columns (`family`,
`link`, `formula`, the complete-case counters, `aic`, `deviance`,
`null_deviance`). The model can be materialized with `CREATE TABLE AS`,
inspected with `SELECT`, joined, compared across refits, and audited —
all with ordinary SQL. `predict_glm()` closes the loop: it consumes the
model relation together with a data relation and returns the data with one
appended `` `<response>_predicted` `` column. As evidence that this
relation is a complete representation of the model rather than a projection
of hidden state, the reference implementation of `predict_glm()` contains
no R at all: it reconstructs the linear predictor and inverse link in
PL/pgSQL purely from the coefficient rows and the metadata column.

Keeping the output to exactly one relation requires two deliberate
denormalizations. First, model-level scalars such as `aic` are repeated on
every row of the coefficient relation. Second, the information needed to
rebuild the design matrix at prediction time — factor levels, contrasts,
term structure — is stored in a `metadata` JSONB column, likewise repeated
on every row. A normalized design would split this into separate
coefficient, model-summary, and metadata relations, but a multi-relation
output would give up precisely the closure property on which composition
rests, so FbSQL accepts the redundancy as an explicit trade-off (revisited
in the *Discussion* section). Unlike a binary model object, the JSONB
metadata remains inspectable from SQL — `metadata -> 'xlevels'` answers
"which factor levels did this model see at training time?" as an ordinary
query.

Existing systems occupy different points on this spectrum rather than
simply lacking the property. Spark MLlib preserves closure at the DataFrame
level rather than the SQL level: `transform()` maps a DataFrame to a
DataFrame, but the model itself stays a host-language object. Hivemall
comes closest to relational closure among the systems we compare — its
trained model is a genuine `(feature, weight)` table — but the features
are integer indices without term semantics, and prediction must be
assembled by hand from an explode, a join against the model table, and an
explicitly written link function. MADlib and PostgresML rely on procedural
interfaces: training writes state under a given name, and prediction is a
scalar function that refers back to it.

## Order independence

Relations are unordered, and a query result carries no row order unless an
`ORDER BY` is written. This principle has two consequences for FbSQL. The
first concerns row order: FbSQL guarantees nothing about the row order of
the fitted-model relation, so `predict_glm()` matches coefficients to terms
by name and cross-checks the set of terms against the `coef_terms` metadata
field, never by position. The contrast is MADlib's model representation,
which stores coefficients, standard errors, and *p* values as parallel
arrays without term names: position is the only semantics, and any
downstream use depends on reconstructing the training-time column order.
The second consequence concerns argument order. Statistical functions
accumulate arguments quickly (`relation`, `formula`, `family`, and in the
future offsets, weights, or tuning parameters), and positional calls become
fragile as the list grows. FbSQL therefore specifies its calls with named
notation — `fit_glm(relation => ..., formula => ..., family => ...)` — so
that invocations are order independent and self-documenting. Named notation
is PostgreSQL (and Oracle) syntax rather than standard SQL; we return to
this limitation in the *Discussion* section.

## NULL semantics

SQL treats NULL as *unknown* under a three-valued logic, and NULLs
propagate through expressions. The statistical counterpart is missing data,
and existing systems answer the question independently of SQL's convention,
each in a different way: PostgresML raises a hard error when a feature is
NULL at prediction time; Spark's default pipeline fails at `transform()`
while its `handleInvalid = "skip"` option silently drops rows, changing the
granularity of the output; H2O-3 [@h2o] imputes column means by default.
Each choice is defensible in its own context, but none follows from the
semantics of the surrounding query language.

FbSQL aligns the two traditions where they already agree. At fitting time
it performs complete-case analysis, exactly matching the `na.omit` default
of R's `glm()`, and reports rather than hides the effect through three
model-level columns (`n_obs`, `n_used`, `n_dropped`). At prediction time a
NULL predictor propagates to a NULL prediction — the result that SQL
expression evaluation of the linear predictor would itself produce, and the
relational rendering of R's `NA` prediction. No value is imputed, no row is
dropped, and no error interrupts the query: the output keeps the
granularity of the input, and NULL means exactly what it means everywhere
else in SQL.

## From glm to a family of modeling functions

The five principles, not the estimator, define FbSQL. `glm()` was chosen as
the proof of concept because it is small enough to specify completely and
rich enough to exercise every principle — formulas, factors, links, missing
data, and inference columns. Nothing in this section is specific to
generalized linear models: for any future `fit_xxx()`, the design question
each principle poses is the same, culminating in what we call the *Minimum
Atomic Relation* question — what is the smallest relation that supports
both the interpretation of a fitted model and the reconstruction of its
predictions? The *Discussion* section sketches answers for tree ensembles
(`fit_rf()`, `fit_lgbm()`, `fit_xgb()`), whose fitted models are naturally
relations over split rules rather than coefficients. The claim of this
paper is the language design; the estimator behind `fit_glm()` is
deliberately ordinary.

# FbSQL Language Design

This section specifies FbSQL as a language: what the user writes and what
the system guarantees. How the reference implementation computes it —
PL/R, testing, packaging — is deferred to the implementation section; here
we describe only what was designed.

## Design goals

Four goals follow directly from the design principles. First, statistical
modeling should be *written as* SQL rather than *called from* SQL: fitting
and prediction are ordinary functions usable inside queries, views, and
`CREATE TABLE AS`, not commands that leave the query language. Second, the
formula is the first-class model-specification construct: the user states
the statistical relationship, and the language defines everything
operational that follows from it. Third, every modeling operation is
relation-in/relation-out. Fourth — the contrapositive of the third — no
model object is ever exposed: the fitted model is data, not a handle. The
proof of concept binds these goals to two functions:

```sql
fbsql.fit_glm(
    relation  text,                    -- SQL string defining the training relation
    formula   text,                    -- R formula, e.g. 'churn_flag ~ age + gender'
    family    text DEFAULT 'gaussian'  -- 'gaussian' | 'binomial'
) RETURNS SETOF fbsql.glm_fit

fbsql.predict_glm(
    relation      text,                -- SQL string defining the relation to score
    model         text,                -- SQL string returning a fitted-model relation
    on_new_levels text DEFAULT 'error' -- 'error' | 'na'
) RETURNS SETOF record
```

Both functions live in the `fbsql` schema; the examples in this paper
assume `SET search_path TO fbsql, public;` and write them unqualified.
Figure 1 gives the overview: every arrow in it is a relation.

<!-- TODO(Figure 1, figures/system_overview.pdf, drawn for the paper):
     data-flow diagram. Left: training relation (any SQL query) + formula +
     family entering fit_glm; center: the 17-column model relation as a
     genuine table; right: model relation + scoring relation entering
     predict_glm, producing the scoring relation + <response>_predicted.
     Annotate that no arrow carries a model object — every edge is a
     relation. -->

## Formula-based model specification

Fitting the running example's churn model is a single query:

```sql
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE logit_model AS
SELECT *
FROM fit_glm(
    relation => $$
        SELECT churn_flag, age, gender
        FROM customer
        WHERE DATE_PART('YEAR', created_at) = 2025
    $$,
    formula => 'churn_flag ~ age + gender',
    family  => 'binomial');
```

The three arguments divide the specification cleanly. `relation` is a SQL
string — dollar-quoted by convention — defining the training data. Because
it may be any query, the modeling layer introduces no data-access
sublanguage of its own: filtering, joining, and feature construction happen
in ordinary SQL before the model ever sees the data, and anything SQL can
express can be trained on. `formula` carries the statistical specification
in the notation of @chambers1992statistical. We adopt it rather than invent
a new syntax for three reasons: it has carried three decades of
statistical-modeling practice, it is compact and declarative — it names a
relationship, not a procedure — and it comes with precise, widely
understood semantics. FbSQL fixes those semantics to be exactly those of
R's `glm()` [@rcore]: character columns become factors with sorted levels,
the first level is the reference under treatment contrasts, and rows with
missing values are dropped as in `na.omit`. Fixing the semantics by
reference to an existing system makes the specification *testable*:
conformance of any implementation can be checked against R output, and the
reference implementation's regression suite does exactly that. The formula
is therefore not merely a string argument; it is the model-specification
sublanguage of the DSL, and the derivation of the design matrix — encoding,
contrasts, reference levels — is defined by the language rather than
performed by the user. `family` names the error distribution, with the
canonical link implied in the proof of concept (gaussian/identity,
binomial/logit).

The call style is part of the specification: FbSQL documents named-argument
invocation (`relation => ...`, `formula => ...`) as its normative form.
Statistical functions accumulate arguments, and named notation keeps calls
order independent and self-documenting as the list grows — the argument-
order half of the order-independence principle. Named notation is
PostgreSQL and Oracle syntax rather than standard SQL, a point we return to
in the *Discussion* section.

## Relation representation of statistical models

The central design decision of FbSQL is what `fit_glm()` returns. It does
not return a handle to a model; it returns the model itself, as a relation
of type `fbsql.glm_fit` with seventeen columns at two granularities:

| Column | Grain | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `term` | term | design matrix column name, as in R's coefficient table |
| `estimate` | term | coefficient |
| `std_error` | term | standard error |
| `statistic` | term | *t* value (gaussian) / *z* value (binomial) |
| `p_value` | term | *p* value |
| `conf_low_95`, `conf_high_95` | term | Wald 95% confidence bounds |
| `family`, `link`, `formula` | model | the specification, echoed back |
| `n_obs`, `n_used`, `n_dropped` | model | complete-case accounting |
| `aic`, `deviance`, `null_deviance` | model | model-level fit measures |
| `metadata` | model | JSONB; everything prediction needs (below) |

One row corresponds to one design matrix column — `(Intercept)`, each
numeric predictor, each non-reference factor level — so the churn model of
Figure 2 is a four-row relation with terms `(Intercept)`, `age`,
`genderM`, and `genderOther`. The term-level column names follow the
vocabulary R users know from model-tidying conventions (`term`,
`estimate`, `std_error`, `statistic`, `p_value`), adjusted to legal SQL
identifiers, so the relation is immediately readable from both sides of
the language boundary.

<!-- TODO(Figure 2, figures/running_example.pdf, drawn for the paper):
     the customer table split by DATE_PART('YEAR', created_at); the 2025
     partition flowing into fit_glm and materializing as logit_model with
     its four coefficient rows sketched; logit_model + the 2026 partition
     flowing into predict_glm and yielding customer_id +
     churn_flag_predicted. -->

Returning a relation rather than an object has three consequences that
motivate the design. First, *composition*: the model can be materialized
(`CREATE TABLE AS`), stored, joined, and filtered like any other data —
two refits can be compared by joining their relations on `term`, and a
model-level quantity is one query away (`SELECT DISTINCT aic FROM
logit_model`). Second, *inspectability*: there is nothing about the fitted
model that `SELECT` cannot show, which contrasts with the serialized or
host-language model representations reviewed in the *Related Work*
section. Third, *implementation independence*: because the language defines
the model as data with specified columns, any implementation — in any
language — that produces and consumes this relation is a conforming one;
the model representation is part of the language, not of the engine.

Two granularities coexist in one relation, and this is deliberate.
Model-level values (`aic`, `n_used`, `family`, ...) are repeated on every
term row — a denormalization, since these columns depend on the model, not
the term. The normalized alternative, splitting the output into coefficient
and model-summary relations, was rejected because a multi-relation return
gives up closure — the property the design exists to preserve. FbSQL
accepts the redundancy and documents it as the explicit price of
one-relation-out; the *Discussion* section weighs this trade-off.

The same reasoning shapes the seventeenth column. A coefficient table alone
is not a complete model representation: reconstructing predictions requires
the factor levels seen at training time, the contrast scheme, the term
structure, and the column types — information that R keeps inside its
model object. FbSQL stores it in a `metadata` JSONB column, repeated on
every row like the other model-level columns. For a model
`y ~ x1 + gender` fitted on a numeric predictor and a three-level factor,
the value is (exactly as produced by the reference implementation):

```json
{
  "meta_version": 1,
  "response": "y",
  "term_labels": ["x1", "gender"],
  "intercept": true,
  "data_classes": {"y": "numeric", "x1": "numeric", "gender": "factor"},
  "xlevels": {"gender": ["F", "M", "Other"]},
  "contrasts": {"gender": "contr.treatment"},
  "coef_terms": ["(Intercept)", "x1", "genderM", "genderOther"]
}
```

The schema is versioned (`meta_version`) because the field layout is part
of the language's API, and it never duplicates the flat columns (`family`,
`formula`, the counters), keeping a single source for each fact. The one
deliberate redundancy is `coef_terms`, the canonical set of coefficient
terms: since row order is never guaranteed, a consumer matches coefficients
by name and uses `coef_terms` to detect missing or duplicated rows.
`xlevels` answers what no coefficient table can — the reference level `F`
appears in no `term` — and enables the unseen-level checks of the next
subsection. Storing this as JSONB is itself a trade-off: it is a departure
from flat relational columns, but it keeps the output to one relation, and
unlike an opaque model object it remains queryable — `metadata ->
'xlevels'` is an ordinary SQL expression. With `metadata`, the relation is
a *self-contained* model representation: nothing needed for prediction
lives outside it.

## Prediction interface

Prediction consumes two relations and returns one:

```sql
SELECT customer_id, churn_flag_predicted
FROM predict_glm(
    relation => $$
        SELECT customer_id, age, gender
        FROM customer
        WHERE DATE_PART('YEAR', created_at) = 2026
    $$,
    model => $$ SELECT * FROM logit_model $$
) AS p(customer_id varchar, age integer, gender varchar,
       churn_flag_predicted double precision);
```

Both inputs are relations on equal footing. The scoring data is a SQL
string as in `fit_glm()`; the model is *also* a SQL string — any query
returning a `glm_fit`-shaped relation qualifies, whether a materialized
table, a view, or a filtered copy. The model is passed as data, not as a
name resolved against system state: there is no registry, no deployment
indirection, and no session dependence — scoring is a pure function of the
two relations. The output is the scoring relation's rows with one appended
column, `` `<response>_predicted` ``, named from the metadata (here
`churn_flag_predicted`, a probability on the response scale). The output
keeps the granularity of the input — one row in, one row out — and carries
no row-order guarantee, per the order-independence principle; coefficients
are matched to terms by name and validated against `coef_terms`, never by
position. One ergonomic cost is visible in the example: because the output
schema is "the input columns plus one derived column", it cannot be
declared statically in PostgreSQL's type system, so `predict_glm()` returns
`SETOF record` and the caller attaches a column definition list. We record
this as a limitation of the current specification and discuss alternatives
in the *Discussion* section.

Missing and unseen values follow the NULL-semantics principle. A NULL
predictor yields a NULL prediction — the row is kept, and NULL means
unknown. Factor levels not seen at training time are governed by
`on_new_levels`, an argument of `predict_glm()` rather than a property of
the model: whether an unknown level should abort the query or produce an
unknown prediction is a decision of the *consumer* of the predictions, not
a fact about the fitted model, so it does not belong in `metadata`. The
default `'error'` fails loudly, naming the offending level and the known
levels from `xlevels`; `'na'` keeps the affected rows with NULL
predictions, preserving output granularity. This design — detection from
stored levels, an explicit caller-side policy, error as the default — is
carried over from fbrglm [@fbrglm], an R package by the first author whose
formula-based interface confronted the same unseen-level problem; FbSQL
restates that answer in relational terms.

## Generalization beyond GLM

Nothing in the interface above is specific to generalized linear models.
The pattern is a pair of conventions — `fit_xxx(relation, formula, ...)`
returning a model relation, and `predict_xxx(relation, model)` returning
the scored relation — plus one design question that each new estimator
family must answer, which we call the *Minimum Atomic Relation* question:

> What is the smallest relation that supports both the interpretation of a
> fitted model and the reconstruction of its predictions?

For GLMs the answer is the seventeen-column relation above: term-grain
coefficient rows, repeated model-level columns, and the metadata needed to
rebuild the design matrix. For tree ensembles — future `fit_rf()`,
`fit_lgbm()`, `fit_xgb()` — the natural grain is not the coefficient but
the split: a model relation whose rows are nodes (tree, node, split
variable, threshold, leaf value), with ensemble-level columns and metadata
playing the same roles as here. The interface conventions — named
arguments, relation-valued model input, `` `<response>_predicted` ``
output, caller-side policies for unseen data — carry over unchanged. The
*Discussion* section sketches these designs; the point of this section is
that the language, not the estimator, is the fixed part of FbSQL.

# PostgreSQL Extension Implementation

<!-- TODO: PL/R for fit (stats::glm as internal engine, never exposed);
     predict in PL/pgSQL without R as evidence the relation is a complete
     model representation; error-handling design (incl. the SPI-abort
     lesson); pg_regress + R-parity testing discipline. -->

# Running Example

This section walks through FbSQL from the user's side: a complete
fit-and-predict workflow written in nothing but SQL. The example is not
illustrative pseudocode — it ships verbatim as a regression test in the
reference implementation (`test/sql/running_example.sql`), and every number
shown below is locked by that suite and verified against R's `glm()` and
`predict.glm()` to four decimals in the companion repository. Figure 2
summarizes the flow: the `customer` table feeds `fit_glm()`, which yields a
model relation; the model relation and the scoring rows feed
`predict_glm()`, which yields a prediction relation.

<!-- TODO(Figure 2 is referenced here and in the Language Design section;
     content spec: customer -> fit_glm -> model relation (logit_model, four
     coefficient rows sketched) -> predict_glm (together with the 2026
     rows) -> prediction relation (customer_id + churn_flag_predicted).
     Asset: figures/running_example.pdf, drawn for the paper. -->

## Dataset

The example uses a single `customer` table with five columns:
`customer_id` (`VARCHAR`), the customer identifier; `created_at`
(`TIMESTAMP`), when the customer signed up; `age` (`INTEGER`) and `gender`
(`VARCHAR`, taking the values `F`, `M`, and `Other` in the training data),
the predictors; and `churn_flag` (`BOOLEAN`), whether the customer churned
— the response. Twelve customers created in 2025 have observed churn
outcomes and serve as training data. Five customers created in 2026 are to
be scored; their `churn_flag` is NULL because the outcome is not yet known.
The 2026 rows deliberately include two realities of production scoring
data: customer `c104` has a NULL `age`, and customer `c105` reports a
gender (`Nonbinary`) that does not occur in the 2025 training data. The
scenario is the everyday one: learn from last year's customers, score this
year's.

<!-- TODO(Table 2): the customer table in full (12 training rows + 5
     scoring rows), flagging the c104 NULL and the c105 unseen level;
     source data is literal in test/sql/running_example.sql and
     FbSQL-experiments/data/customer.csv. -->

## Model fitting

Fitting the churn model is one SQL statement:

```sql
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE logit_model AS
SELECT *
FROM fit_glm(
    relation => $$
        SELECT churn_flag, age, gender
        FROM customer
        WHERE DATE_PART('YEAR', created_at) = 2025
    $$,
    formula => 'churn_flag ~ age + gender',
    family  => 'binomial');
```

The three arguments carry the three concerns of the *Language Design*
section. `relation` selects the training data with ordinary SQL — here, the
2025 cohort; any filtering or feature construction would go in this query.
`formula` states the model: churn as a function of age and gender.
`family` picks logistic regression. Everything operational happens without
user involvement: `gender` becomes a factor with reference level `F`, and
the boolean response is accepted directly. The result is a table like any
other, and selecting from it shows the fitted model:

```sql
SELECT term, round(estimate::numeric, 4) AS estimate,
       round(std_error::numeric, 4) AS std_error,
       family, link, n_obs, n_used, n_dropped
FROM logit_model ORDER BY term;
```
```
    term     | estimate | std_error |  family  | link  | n_obs | n_used | n_dropped
-------------+----------+-----------+----------+-------+-------+--------+-----------
 (Intercept) | -12.1071 |    7.1895 | binomial | logit |    12 |     12 |         0
 age         |   0.2981 |    0.1669 | binomial | logit |    12 |     12 |         0
 genderM     |  -0.4305 |    2.5525 | binomial | logit |    12 |     12 |         0
 genderOther |  -0.7049 |    2.8735 | binomial | logit |    12 |     12 |         0
```

One row per term, exactly as R would report it, with the model-level
columns repeated on each row; the relation also carries the `metadata`
column of the *Language Design* section, to which we return below.

## Prediction

Scoring the 2026 customers consumes two relations — the data to score and
the model, both passed as queries — and returns a relation:

```sql
SELECT customer_id, round(churn_flag_predicted::numeric, 4) AS churn_flag_predicted
FROM predict_glm(
    relation => $$
        SELECT customer_id, age, gender
        FROM customer
        WHERE DATE_PART('YEAR', created_at) = 2026
          AND customer_id <> 'c105'
    $$,
    model => $$ SELECT * FROM logit_model $$
) AS p(customer_id varchar, age integer, gender varchar,
       churn_flag_predicted double precision)
ORDER BY customer_id;
```
```
 customer_id | churn_flag_predicted
-------------+----------------------
 c101        |               0.0406
 c102        |               0.9794
 c103        |               0.4280
 c104        |
```

The model argument deserves a second look: `$$ SELECT * FROM logit_model $$`
passes the fitted model *as data*. Any query returning a model-shaped
relation would do; there is no model registry to consult and no name to
resolve. The output is the input relation's rows plus one column,
`churn_flag_predicted` — a churn probability, named after the response in
the formula. Customer `c104`, whose `age` is NULL, is neither dropped nor
rejected: the row is kept and its prediction is NULL, which is what NULL
means in SQL — unknown in, unknown out.

Customer `c105` was excluded above because its `gender` value `Nonbinary`
never occurred in the training data, and under the default policy scoring
it stops the query with an explicit error:

```
ERROR:  predict_glm: factor 'gender' has new level 'Nonbinary' not seen at
fit time (known levels: ["F", "M", "Other"]); use on_new_levels => 'na' to
return NULL for such rows
```

Passing `on_new_levels => 'na'` instead keeps all five rows and returns a
NULL prediction for `c105` alone. The two policies reflect one design idea:
what to do with an unseen level is the *caller's* decision, not the
model's, so it is an argument of `predict_glm()`. The default fails loudly
— silent number-producing behaviors for unseen categories are among the
differences documented in the *Related Work* comparison — and the `'na'`
alternative preserves the output granularity while marking exactly the
affected rows as unknown.

## Inspecting the fitted model

Because the fitted model is a relation, everything a user might ask of it
is a query, not a method call. Model-level quantities are one `SELECT`
away:

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT aic, deviance, null_deviance FROM logit_model;
```

The training-time conditions travel with the model in its `metadata`
column and answer questions the coefficient table cannot. Which factor
levels did the model see? —

```sql
SELECT DISTINCT metadata -> 'xlevels' FROM logit_model;
```
```
 {"gender": ["F", "M", "Other"]}
```

The reference level `F` appears in no coefficient row, yet it is right
there in `xlevels`; this is also precisely the information behind the
unseen-level error above. And because the model is a relation, it composes
with SQL itself. Refitting next year's model and joining it against this
year's compares two models term by term, with no export step and no
model-object API:

```sql
SELECT a.term, a.estimate AS estimate_2025, b.estimate AS estimate_2026
FROM logit_model AS a
JOIN logit_model_2026 AS b USING (term);
```

Nothing in this section serialized an object, called a method, or consulted
system state: the model was created by a query, stored as a table, passed
to prediction as a query, and audited by queries.

## Summary

The workflow was four SQL statements: create the model relation, read it,
score with it, and inspect it. Both `fit_glm()` and `predict_glm()`
consumed relations and produced relations; the model itself was a table
throughout its life; and at no point did the user touch anything but SQL.
Note also what the walkthrough did *not* depend on: no step revealed — or
needed to reveal — what computes behind the two functions. That opacity is
the language boundary working as designed, and it is what makes the
implementation replaceable. The *PostgreSQL Extension Implementation*
section describes the reference implementation on the other side of that
boundary, including the fact that prediction runs without R at all — a
direct consequence of the model relation being, as the *Language Design*
section specified, a self-contained representation.

# Experimental Evaluation

<!-- TODO: design comparison built from FbSQL-experiments (running example
     reproduced on MADlib / PostgresML / Spark MLlib; numeric parity where
     the estimator allows it; safety differences: silent reference-level
     scoring, hard NULL errors, dropped rows, non-R RFormula semantics). -->

# Discussion

<!-- TODO: trade-offs (denormalized model-level columns, jsonb metadata,
     SETOF record column definition lists); generalization to tree
     ensembles (fit_rf / fit_lgbm / fit_xgb atomic relations); C
     implementation outlook; named arguments and the SQL standard;
     deliberate exclusions (weights/offset). -->

# Conclusion

<!-- TODO: restate the claim: a statistical modeling language faithful to
     SQL design principles, with glm as the first proof of concept. -->

<!-- Computational details / Acknowledgments / Replication material
     sections to be added in JSS style at writing time. -->
